summary: index-ulid ships a postinstall dropper with cross-platform persistence and WebSocket C2
details: Package masquerades as a ULID generator (typosquat of ulidx — rollup config builds with `--name ulidx` and the README references the legitimate ulid/ulidx libraries) but its npm postinstall hook unconditionally executes dist/node/utils.js, which copies the bundled 953 KB dist/node/payload.js into a hidden directory named 'MicrosoftSystem64' under %LOCALAPPDATA% / ~/.config / Application Support and launches it detached as a long-lived agent (`spawn(process.execPath, [script, '--bg'], { detached: true })`). The dropper installs cross-platform persistence: on Windows a scheduled task `\MicrosoftSystem64` registered via schtasks at ONLOGON with a wscript VBS launcher and an HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run fallback; on Linux a `~/.config/systemd/user/MicrosoftSystem64.service` user unit plus `loginctl enable-linger` and a `~/.config/autostart/MicrosoftSystem64.desktop` fallback; on macOS a detached node child. The agent (payload.js) imports the `ws` WebSocket client for C2, references huggingface.co / HF_API as a dead-drop channel, and includes sandbox-evasion gates (`MIN_CPU_COUNT = 5` in payload.js and `cpus.length <= 4 ||!cpus[0]?.model` in utils.js refuse to run on small machines/CI). None of this behavior is documented and is unrelated to ID generation. Every installer running `npm install index-ulid` is compromised at install time with a persistent backdoor.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
